chore(beads): flush issue tracker updates

@@ -217,7 +217,7 @@
 {"id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.7.4","title":"T6.7.7.4 MVCC-native snapshot publication plane for pager metadata and page visibility","description":"Introduce an MVCC-native snapshot publication plane for pager metadata and page-visibility state. Instead of forcing steady-state readers and write staging through shared ownership of coarse pager state, publish reader-consumable snapshots or generation-stamped metadata views using an evidence-backed mechanism such as partitioned ownership, epoch/RCU-style publication, seqlock-style publication, or an equivalent design.

This is the architectural jump from "less locking" to "structurally cheap reads and visibility checks." The exact primitive is not pre-selected, but the outcome is: readers should bind to a coherent published view of relevant pager state without paying hidden global-ownership tax.","acceptance_criteria":"1. Steady-state readers can consult a published pager/visibility snapshot or equivalent metadata view without taking the old global ownership path for ordinary access.
2. Publication invariants are explicit for commit visibility, page-state versioning, checkpoint boundaries, and stale-reader retry behavior.
3. Deterministic unit/property tests cover monotonic publication, stale/superseded reader views, retry behavior, ABA-like hazards, and reclamation safety when applicable.
4. Provide `scripts/verify_t6_7_snapshot_publication.sh` to exercise file-backed concurrent-reader/concurrent-writer scenarios with fixed seeds, deterministic schedules where available, and fallback rejection enabled.
5. Structured logs emit `trace_id`, `run_id`, `scenario_id`, `snapshot_gen`, `visible_commit_seq`, `publication_mode`, `read_retry_count`, `page_set_size`, `elapsed_ns`, and first-failure diagnostics.","notes":"Future-self intent:
- This bead is deliberately frontier-leaning. It is the place to use serious publication techniques if the evidence supports them.
- Do not reduce it to a thinner mutex wrapper; the whole point is a genuinely published metadata plane that makes MVCC visible in the physical runtime structure.
- User-facing payoff: lower read inflation during write pressure, better fairness, and a storage story that is truly differentiated from SQLite's serialized guts.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"MossyTiger","estimated_minutes":420,"created_at":"2026-03-06T06:45:57.056859619Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-10T23:31:08.836645258Z","closed_at":"2026-03-10T23:31:08.833800822Z","close_reason":"Live-tree snapshot publication plane verified: dedicated gate passes; rch cargo check passes; remaining workspace clippy/fmt failures are unrelated branch drift","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-uplift","architecture-audit-2026-03","mvcc","next-gen-architecture","optimization","pager","performance","promised-land","storage-wiring","track6","verification"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.7.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.7","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-12T00:25:42Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.7.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.7.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-12T00:25:42Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":2190,"issue_id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.7.4","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Design Review Required (2026-03-08 quality audit)

### Issue: Mechanism Not Finalized
This bead lists design OPTIONS but no DECISION:
- "Partitioned ownership" — epoch-based page ownership per connection
- "Epoch/RCU-style" — read-copy-update for metadata reads
- "Seqlock" — sequence-locked reads for metadata snapshots

### Recommendation
Schedule explicit design review BEFORE implementation begins. The choice affects:
- bd-8.4 (WAL publication plane) which depends on this
- bd-13.3 (hot-page deflection) which is already over-constrained
- bd-9.4 (adaptive fairness) which also depends on this

### Design Decision Criteria
1. Must support concurrent readers without blocking writers (MVCC invariant)
2. Must be compatible with cross-process SharedTxnSlot atomics (no process-local locks)
3. Must not increase commit latency by > 5% (benchmark evidence required)
4. Must integrate with asupersync Cx cancellation (bracket-safe reads)

### Suggested Approach
RCU-style with generation counters is most natural for MVCC metadata:
- Writers publish new metadata under a new generation
- Readers snapshot the current generation at transaction start
- Old generations reclaimed after all readers using them complete
This aligns with existing MVCC snapshot semantics.
","created_at":"2026-03-08T00:20:49Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.8","title":"T6.7.8 Make WAL page indexing authoritative and bound refresh/read cost","description":"`fsqlite-wal` still pays too much lookup and refresh tax because the WAL index is not yet authoritative enough for the steady-state path. Reverse scans, capped indexing behavior, and refresh-oriented compensation logic make the runtime pay repeatedly for uncertainty that should instead be resolved once in the write/publication path.

This bead is therefore more than a localized WAL micro-optimization. It is the move that turns WAL metadata into a first-class part of the concurrency architecture: authoritative, incrementally maintained, and cheap for readers and commit coordination to consult without rediscovering state on the hot path.","acceptance_criteria":"1. The steady-state WAL read path no longer depends on reverse scans or broad refresh work to resolve page/frame visibility under normal operation.
2. WAL metadata becomes authoritative enough that readers and commit/checkpoint coordination can consult it directly with bounded lookup cost and explicit invariants.
3. Deterministic tests and failure-injection suites cover truncation, torn tail, corruption, recovery, and incremental refresh behavior without semantic drift.
4. Benchmarks from T6.7.1 show reduced WAL lookup/refresh tax and clearer attribution of remaining write-path cost.","notes":"Execution split:
- `bd-1dp9.6.7.8.1`: generation/index invariants and authoritative lookup contract.
- `bd-1dp9.6.7.8.4`: commit-published WAL visibility plane.
- `bd-1dp9.6.7.8.2`: incremental refresh / steady-state lookup cutover.
- `bd-1dp9.6.7.8.3`: corruption/truncate/torn-tail evidence pack.

Planning intent:
- Treat this as concurrency-substrate work, not just a read-side cleanup task.
- The long-term goal is that WAL metadata publication becomes something the rest of the engine can trust structurally, rather than revalidating through hot-path scans.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","estimated_minutes":600,"created_at":"2026-03-06T04:02:05.040972610Z","created_by":"Codex","updated_at":"2026-03-06T06:46:04.632424916Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["architecture-audit-2026-03","optimization","performance","storage-wiring","track6","wal"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.8","depends_on_id":"bd-1dp9.6.7","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-08T21:54:22Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.8.1","title":"T6.7.8.1 WAL generation/index invariants and authoritative lookup contract","description":"Define and instrument the correctness contract for an authoritative WAL page index per WAL generation. This subtask makes reset/truncate/ABA/torn-tail behavior explicit so the optimization does not blur exceptional recovery conditions into the steady-state fast path.","acceptance_criteria":"1. WAL generation/index invariants are explicit for steady-state, reset/truncate, ABA, torn-tail, and corruption/recovery cases.
2. Instrumentation and deterministic tests validate generation identity, invalidation, and lookup-contract behavior.
3. The contract distinguishes normal authoritative-index lookup from exceptional fallback/recovery handling.
4. Future implementers can reason about the correctness envelope without re-deriving WAL semantics from code archaeology.","notes":"AmberOx (Codex): claiming task and beginning implementation on WAL generation/index invariants + instrumentation + deterministic tests.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":210,"created_at":"2026-03-06T04:14:07.018609059Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-09T23:34:23.321628061Z","closed_at":"2026-04-09T23:34:23.321408419Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["architecture-audit-2026-03","optimization","performance","storage-wiring","track6","wal"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.8.1","depends_on_id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.1.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-08T21:54:22Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.8.1","depends_on_id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.8","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-08T21:54:22Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":2419,"issue_id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.8.1","author":"codex","text":"2026-03-16 Codex audit: resetting from in_progress to open. The current tree does contain WAL generation-identity and lookup-contract code/tests in wal.rs and wal_adapter.rs, but this bead itself only records the initial claim note and does not carry a close-quality evidence comment establishing that the full contract/instrumentation slice was finished. That means the issue should not continue to look actively owned.","created_at":"2026-03-16T23:20:10Z"}]}
-{"id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.8.2","title":"T6.7.8.2 Incremental refresh and steady-state lookup cutover off reverse scans","description":"Implement the authoritative WAL index and incremental refresh path so normal reads no longer degrade into reverse frame scans and refresh cost scales with newly appended frames. The design must preserve correct handling of generation changes and damaged tails.","acceptance_criteria":"1. Steady-state latest-page lookup uses the authoritative index for the current WAL generation.
2. Refresh work scales with appended frames and generation changes instead of repeated full-log scan/parse behavior.
3. Deterministic tests cover append/refresh concurrency, generation resets, and correctness under edge conditions defined in the invariants bead.
4. Structured logs expose lookup mode, refresh deltas, generation changes, and fallback/recovery reasons.","notes":"User-facing payoff:
- Read latency and read-amid-write behavior become more predictable because normal WAL lookup stops paying hidden scan tax.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":300,"created_at":"2026-03-06T04:14:07.743787496Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-06T06:51:40.554439082Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["architecture-audit-2026-03","optimization","performance","storage-wiring","track6","wal"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.8.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.8","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-08T21:54:22Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.8.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.8.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-08T21:54:22Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

The design must preserve correct handling of generation changes and damaged tails.","acceptance_criteria":"1. 2. Refresh work scales with appended frames and generation changes instead of repeated full-log scan/parse behavior.
3. Deterministic tests cover append/refresh concurrency, generation resets, and correctness under edge conditions defined in the invariants bead.
4. Structured logs expose lookup mode, refresh deltas, generation changes, and fallback/recovery reasons.","notes":"User-facing payoff:
- Read latency and read-amid-write behavior become more predictable because normal WAL lookup stops paying hidden scan tax.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":300,"created_at":"2026-03-06T04:14:07.743787496Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-15T03:58:51.528243385Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["architecture-audit-2026-03","optimization","performance","storage-wiring","track6","wal"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.8.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.8","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-08T21:54:22Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.8.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.8.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-08T21:54:22Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.8.3","title":"T6.7.8.3 WAL truncate/torn-tail/corruption suite with authoritative-index trace pack","description":"Build the proof bundle for authoritative WAL indexing: deterministic edge-case tests, corruption/truncate/torn-tail scenarios, and structured logs proving the fast path is used only when generation semantics are sound.","acceptance_criteria":"1. Deterministic tests cover truncate/reset, torn tails, corruption/recovery entry, and concurrent append/refresh scenarios.
2. Structured logs expose generation identity, lookup mode, recovery/fallback reasons, and first-failure diagnostics.
3. Replay instructions and artifact hashes are published and tied back to the T6.7.1 artifact contract.
4. The evidence pack demonstrates reduced lookup/refresh cost in steady state without hiding exceptional cases.","notes":"Verification blueprint:
- Unit/property layer:
  - Cover WAL generation identity, authoritative index invalidation, append/refresh concurrency, truncate/reset, torn tails, and corruption/recovery entry conditions.
- E2E/replay layer:
  - Provide `scripts/verify_t6_7_wal_index.sh` for steady-state lookup, write-heavy refresh, truncate/reset, torn-tail simulation, and corruption-triggered fallback behavior.
- Logging contract:
  - Emit `trace_id`, `run_id`, `scenario_id`, `wal_generation`, `lookup_mode`, `refresh_delta_frames`, `fallback_reason`, `recovery_mode`, `elapsed_ns`, and first-failure diagnostics.
- User-facing intent:
  - Read-path predictability should improve, while exceptional WAL states remain explicit instead of silently blended into the hot path.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":180,"created_at":"2026-03-06T04:14:08.462162169Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-06T04:20:15.557025986Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["architecture-audit-2026-03","optimization","performance","storage-wiring","track6","verification","wal"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.8.3","depends_on_id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.1.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-08T21:54:22Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.8.3","depends_on_id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.8","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-08T21:54:22Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.8.3","depends_on_id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.8.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-08T21:54:22Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.8.4","title":"T6.7.8.4 Commit-published WAL visibility map and generation snapshots","description":"Move WAL metadata publication into the commit path so readers and commit/checkpoint coordination can bind to a commit-published visibility map plus generation snapshot rather than rediscovering the same truth through scans and refresh work. Concretely, writers should publish enough authoritative latest-frame metadata per generation that steady-state readers can perform bounded lookup against a coherent snapshot.

This is the WAL-side counterpart to the pager publication bead: not just faster lookup, but a structural change in where truth is established and how the rest of the engine consumes it.","acceptance_criteria":"1. Commit publication incrementally maintains an authoritative WAL visibility structure for the active generation, sufficient for bounded latest-frame lookup in steady-state reads.
2. Readers bind to a generation-stamped metadata snapshot and do not rely on reverse scans for ordinary visibility resolution.
3. Deterministic unit/property tests cover generation rollover, truncate/reset, torn tail, corruption, recovery, and snapshot coherence under append pressure.
4. Provide `scripts/verify_t6_7_wal_publication_plane.sh` to run file-backed append/read/checkpoint scenarios with fixed seeds and validate both semantics and metadata-publication behavior.
5. Structured logs emit `trace_id`, `run_id`, `scenario_id`, `wal_generation`, `publication_seq`, `frame_delta_count`, `latest_frame_entries`, `snapshot_age`, `lookup_mode`, `fallback_reason`, and first-failure diagnostics.","notes":"Future-self intent:
- Treat this as publication architecture, not a lookup-table convenience feature.
- The win is not only speed; it is that the rest of the runtime can trust WAL truth as a published substrate instead of repeatedly reconstructing it.
- User-facing payoff: more predictable read-amid-write behavior, lower WAL-induced variance, and cleaner diagnosis when recovery modes are actually invoked.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":360,"created_at":"2026-03-06T06:45:57.818991776Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-10T22:38:01.332072350Z","closed_at":"2026-04-10T22:38:01.331821541Z","close_reason":"Implementation landed in 1577f3ce; verification passed on 2026-04-10","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-uplift","architecture-audit-2026-03","next-gen-architecture","optimization","performance","promised-land","storage-wiring","track6","verification","wal"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.8.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.7.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-08T21:54:22Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.8.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.8","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-08T21:54:22Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.8.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.8.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-08T21:54:22Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":261,"issue_id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.8.4","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Design Review Required (2026-03-08 quality audit)

### Issue: WAL Publication Mechanism Undefined
Description says "move WAL metadata publication into the commit path" but doesn't specify the concrete mechanism. This bead depends on bd-1dp9.6.7.7.4 (pager snapshot plane) — the same design review should cover both.

### Design Relationship with 7.4
- 7.4 defines HOW pager metadata is published (RCU/seqlock/partitioned)
- 8.4 defines WHAT WAL metadata is published (visibility map, generation snapshots)
- These should be designed together as a unified publication framework

### Suggested Approach
Extend the generation-counter RCU from 7.4 to cover WAL visibility:
- Commit publishes {commit_seq, affected_pages, wal_frame_range} atomically
- Readers see a consistent snapshot of WAL state at their transaction's generation
- Eliminates reverse scans of WAL index for page visibility checks
","created_at":"2026-03-08T00:20:56Z"},{"id":2266,"issue_id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.8.4","author":"MistyWolf","text":"Verification update from MistyWolf on 2026-03-12:

- `./scripts/verify_t6_7_wal_publication_plane.sh` passed end to end with `CORE_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/bd_1dp9_6_7_8_4_core_wal_publication` and `WAL_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/bd_1dp9_6_7_8_4_wal_publication`.
- Artifact bundle: `artifacts/bd-1dp9.6.7.8.4/bd-1dp9.6.7.8.4-20260312T065941Z-784`.
- The script now hard-fails on missing trace-contract fields and passed with the expected publication/read fields present.
- Verified phases: commit publication path, prepared append publication path, reader snapshot pinning, lookup contract fallback/authoritative split, same-salt generation invalidation, and WAL-side reset/generation detection.
- Key trace evidence emitted during successful runs includes `wal_generation`, `publication_seq`, `frame_delta_count`, `latest_frame_entries`, `snapshot_age`, `lookup_mode`, `fallback_reason`, and reader-side `wal_checkpoint_seq` where required.
- Agent Mail write operations are still intermittently returning `Resource is temporarily busy`, so coordination evidence is being recorded here in Beads until the write queue recovers.","created_at":"2026-03-12T07:14:26Z"},{"id":3520,"issue_id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.8.4","author":"ubuntu","text":"## bd-1dp9.6.7.8.4 progress

- Read `/data/projects/frankensqlite/AGENTS.md` and confirmed the hard rules: keep `concurrent_mode_default` true, no `unsafe`, no Tokio, manual edits only, and no file deletion.
- Ran `br show bd-1dp9.6.7.8.4` and reviewed the bead scope, dependencies, acceptance criteria, and prior verification notes.
- Inspected the existing WAL publication implementation in `crates/fsqlite-core/src/wal_adapter.rs` and verified that it already publishes generation-stamped visibility snapshots from the commit path and binds readers to pinned snapshots.
- Ran `./scripts/verify_t6_7_wal_publication_plane.sh` successfully on 2026-04-10.
  - Fresh artifact bundle: `artifacts/bd-1dp9.6.7.8.4/bd-1dp9.6.7.8.4-20260410T173120Z-784`
  - Verified phases: commit publication, prepared-append publication, reader snapshot binding, partial-index lookup contract, same-salt generation rollover, and WAL-side generation detection.
- Extended the evidence with targeted recovery tests:
  - `rch exec -- cargo test -p fsqlite-harness --test bd_bca_1_phase5_compliance test_wal_recovery_stops_at_first_invalid_frame -- --nocapture`
  - `rch exec -- cargo test -p fsqlite-harness --test bd_bca_1_phase5_compliance test_wal_recovery_torn_write -- --nocapture`
  - Both passed on 2026-04-10.
- Conclusion: in the current checkout, the bead’s WAL publication implementation and verification surface are already passing; no Rust code patch was required during this session.
","created_at":"2026-04-10T17:52:05Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.9","title":"T6.7.9 Group-commit physical writer and checkpoint decoupling","description":"The architecture audit confirmed that physical commit remains serialized even after logical MVCC validation, and the repository already contains throughput-oriented group-commit machinery that does not appear to be the default mainline runtime path. Today the engine still pays too much wall-clock time in the physical writer path, and checkpoint/control work shows up in profiles often enough to matter.

This bead activates a real physical-writer/group-commit lane. The design target is: logical transaction validation and conflict handling can progress independently, while WAL append/fsync and checkpoint interaction happen through a deterministic physical writer that batches where safe. The outcome must preserve commit ordering, crash/recovery semantics, FCW/SSI correctness, and backend identity clarity.","acceptance_criteria":"1. The runtime uses an explicit physical-writer/group-commit path for WAL append/fsync work, and logical commit preparation is no longer forced to wait on every physical write step.
2. Grouped physical commit ordering, checkpoint interaction, and crash/recovery behavior are deterministic and covered by unit + e2e evidence.
3. The write path exposes explicit fairness/latency controls or service-policy rules rather than relying on opaque batching heuristics.
4. Structured logs expose batch composition, fsync boundaries, checkpoint interaction, service-policy decisions, and first-failure diagnostics for grouped-commit scenarios.
5. Benchmarks show measurable improvement on multi-writer workloads without regressing correctness, fairness, or durability guarantees.","notes":"Execution split:
- `bd-1dp9.6.7.9.1`: batching lane and ordering semantics.
- `bd-1dp9.6.7.9.2`: checkpoint decoupling.
- `bd-1dp9.6.7.9.4`: bounded-latency service policy and adaptive fairness control.
- `bd-1dp9.6.7.9.3`: durability/fairness/failure-injection evidence.

Planning intent:
- The ambitious target here is not just "some batching" but a physical commit lane with explicit service objectives and diagnosable control over throughput/fairness tradeoffs.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"feature","estimated_minutes":720,"created_at":"2026-03-06T04:02:05.882152577Z","created_by":"Codex","updated_at":"2026-03-06T06:46:05.002193225Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["architecture-audit-2026-03","checkpoint","group-commit","optimization","performance","track6","wal"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.9","depends_on_id":"bd-1dp9.6.7","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-08T21:54:22Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
@@ -1283,7 +1283,7 @@
 {"id":"bd-3wop3.1","title":"D1: replace centralized WAL append or publish with per-core lanes and tiny ordered durability residue","description":"## Goal
Replace the current centralized WAL append or publish shape with a genuinely parallel WAL design that preserves determinism and crash safety while letting multi-writer throughput scale. The key move is not merely "more batching"; it is to make append staging per-core or per-lane, keep durability publication tiny and deterministic, and publish enough authoritative metadata at commit time that readers and checkpoint logic stop reconstructing truth expensively.

## Why This Matters Now
The current many-core story is better than it was, but the engine is still paying too much for centralized durability/publication choreography. If we want to preserve the existing `c8` wins and drag that advantage down toward `c4`, the WAL path needs to stop acting like a mostly centralized resource with nicer batching.

## Target Architecture
1. Per-core or per-lane append staging for ordinary frame construction.
2. A tiny durability combiner that assigns commit certificates or equivalent ordering tokens and handles the irreducible ordered portion explicitly.
3. Commit-published visibility metadata that readers and checkpoint logic can bind to directly.
4. Recovery and checkpoint semantics that replay or merge the parallel WAL shape deterministically.
5. A conservative fallback or safe-mode path for debugging, recovery-mode diagnosis, and mechanical proof comparisons.

## Scope
- Parallel append staging and local buffering.
- Commit-order and durability-certificate design.
- Visibility publication handoff to the read side.
- Recovery, checkpoint, truncate/reset, and torn-tail semantics.
- Matrix and failure-injection verification.

## Non-Goals
- This is not permission to weaken WAL durability guarantees.
- This is not permission to make commit ordering ambiguous or architecture-specific.
- This is not a speculative controller-first bead; any adaptive batching or flush policy must be subordinate to a deterministic conservative mode.","acceptance_criteria":"1. The production WAL path no longer depends on one effectively centralized append or publish region for ordinary multi-writer throughput.
2. The design names the exact ordered residue that remains and why it cannot be eliminated, rather than hiding it inside a broad lock or opaque combiner.
3. Commit-published visibility metadata is authoritative enough that readers and checkpoint coordination can consume it directly with bounded cost.
4. Recovery, reset, truncate, torn-tail, and checkpoint behavior remain explicitly specified and covered by deterministic tests plus failure-injection runs.
5. Before/after artifacts exist for weak and strong matrix cells so we can see both low-thread overhead and high-thread scalability effects.","notes":"Future-self intent:
- The right end state is not "one lock but faster." The right end state is parallel append staging plus a very small, very explicit ordered durability residue.
- Treat commit-publication metadata as part of the WAL architecture, not as an afterthought.

Operator sequencing:
- start with `bd-3wop3.1.1` for the contract
- then `bd-3wop3.1.2`, `bd-3wop3.1.3`, `bd-3wop3.1.4`, and `bd-3wop3.1.5`
- do not count this container bead as done until recovery, failure-injection, and matrix attribution are all green","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","estimated_minutes":960,"created_at":"2026-03-20T18:31:35.191386524Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-22T23:35:43.472609480Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["contention","phase1","track-d","wal"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3wop3.1","depends_on_id":"bd-3wop3","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-20T18:31:35.191386524Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-3wop3.1.1","title":"D1.a: per-core parallel WAL design contract with commit certificates and safe-mode fallback","description":"## Goal
Define the production-shape parallel WAL design before implementation hardens around the wrong abstraction.

## Required Design Output
Specify:
- the deterministic data-plane contract for the parallel WAL path: the unit of parallel append staging, how commit order is certified or recorded, what minimal ordered durability residue remains and why, how commit-published visibility metadata is produced, and how recovery/checkpoint consume the new shape,
- what conservative fallback or safe-mode path exists for debugging and comparison,
- the explicit operator control surface for `auto`, conservative/safe mode, lane-count override, and any shadow-compare or dual-validation mode,
- if any adaptive batching, flush timing, lane budgeting, or helper-lane policy exists, the decision-plane contract for that logic: state space, action set, loss model or equivalent decision rule, calibration telemetry, budgeted mode caps, and precise fallback trigger,
- a timescale-separation statement describing how any D1 controller composes with E4 admission control, E6 placement policy, and any later cache/routing controller.

## Alien-Artifact Requirements
Produce explicit runtime artifacts rather than hand-wavy architecture text:
1. commit-certificate record format or equivalent proof object,
2. state/action table for any optional batching or flush policy,
3. invariants ledger for ordering, durability, visibility, replay, and control-mode equivalence,
4. fallback triggers that force conservative mode when evidence goes bad,
5. a control-surface schema naming how operators/tests force `auto`, conservative, and shadow-compare modes,
6. a logging-contract schema naming the fields every lane, combiner, checkpoint, recovery, and control-mode trace must expose,
7. if a controller exists, a decision-record schema carrying `policy_id`, `decision_id`, top evidence terms, confidence or posterior, expected loss, counterfactual action plus regret delta, fallback-active bit, and policy version.

## Required Verification Plan
- table-driven validation of commit-order and certificate semantics,
- replay scenarios for lane reordering, checkpoint overlap, reset, recovery, and forced conservative mode,
- explicit verification of any controller fallback, shadow-run, and calibration-drain behavior if the design includes adaptive policy,
- a named design-validation surface such as `scripts/verify_d1_parallel_wal_design_contract.sh` that proves the design can be checked mechanically.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The design makes commit ordering deterministic and auditable.
- The exact irreducible ordered residue is named explicitly.
- Safe-mode fallback and the operator control surface are concrete and testable.
- Recovery and checkpoint semantics are part of the design, not deferred wishful thinking.
- Any decision-plane logic is explicitly separable from the deterministic data plane and can be disabled without losing correctness.
- The verification and logging contracts are explicit enough to bind later implementation beads.","acceptance_criteria":"1. The design artifact includes the commit-certificate format, ordered-residue definition, fallback triggers, logging schema, replay plan, and any decision-record schema required for adaptive policy.
2. Commit ordering, visibility, checkpoint, and recovery semantics are specified precisely enough that later implementation cannot reinterpret them arbitrarily.
3. A named validation surface such as `scripts/verify_d1_parallel_wal_design_contract.sh` is specified with scenarios and expected outputs.
4. If adaptive batching, lane budgeting, or flush policy exists, the design names its conservative baseline, policy artifact identity, calibration/fallback trigger, and timescale-separation assumptions.
5. The bead is self-contained enough to serve as the reference contract for the later D1 implementation and proof beads.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-22T23:11:04.156648411Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-10T02:24:23.426075238Z","closed_at":"2026-04-10T02:24:23.425778667Z","close_reason":"Design contract doc updated with calibration telemetry and fallback semantics","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-artifact-coding","contention","design","performance","track-d","wal"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3wop3.1.1","depends_on_id":"bd-3wop3.1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-22T23:11:04.156648411Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3wop3.1.1","depends_on_id":"bd-db300.7.5.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-23T00:16:14.268122498Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3wop3.1.1","depends_on_id":"bd-db300.7.6.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-23T00:07:27.223541485Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-3wop3.1.2","title":"D1.b: implement per-core append lanes and local frame staging","description":"## Goal
Move ordinary WAL frame staging off the centralized append path and into stable per-core or per-lane structures.

## Scope
- introduce the chosen lane identity and ownership model,
- stage frame construction and buffering locally,
- eliminate the current effectively global append gate from the ordinary write path,
- emit per-lane telemetry for backlog, flush size, and staging cost,
- provide an explicit operator/test control surface for `auto`, conservative single-lane or serialized staging, and any shadow lane-mapping validation mode.

## Required Verification and Logging
- Unit tests for lane identity stability, per-lane buffering behavior, append ordering within a lane, non-blocking ordinary append staging, and forced conservative-mode entry.
- Integration tests for concurrent writers hitting disjoint lanes, lane reuse after worker churn, control-mode equivalence, and safe-mode fallback entry.
- Compatibility reruns for the WAL/write-path surfaces selected by `G5.3`, not only throughput-focused workloads.
- A named e2e script such as `scripts/verify_d1_parallel_wal_staging.sh` that stresses lane-local append staging under canonical workloads and emits first-failure bundles.
- Structured logs exposing at minimum `trace_id`, `scenario_id`, `wal_lane_id`, `lane_backlog`, `staged_frame_count`, `flush_trigger`, `control_mode`, `lane_policy_version`, `shadow_verdict`, `compatibility_selector`, `fallback_reason`, and per-lane elapsed nanoseconds.

## Constraints
- lane identity must not silently depend on unstable thread IDs unless the design explicitly proves that acceptable,
- commit order and visibility remain external to this bead's local staging work,
- ordinary writes should no longer need one centralized append lock simply to construct or queue frames,
- control-mode overrides must preserve WAL/user-visible semantics even when they intentionally route through a conservative path.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The common multi-writer append path uses local lane staging.
- Centralized append serialization is removed or reduced to the tiny ordered residue named by the design contract.
- The implementation is benchmarkable and diagnosable with per-lane metrics and explicit control-mode traces.
- Conservative-mode and shadow-compare paths exist for operator diagnosis and semantic comparison.
- Unit, integration, compatibility, and named e2e verification all cover the touched staging semantics.","acceptance_criteria":"1. Lane-local append staging exists in production code with explicit ownership and telemetry.
2. Unit and integration tests cover lane identity, buffering, same-lane ordering, writer churn, conservative-mode routing, and control-mode equivalence.
3. `scripts/verify_d1_parallel_wal_staging.sh` or equivalent named e2e coverage exists, emits replayable artifacts, and includes the relevant compatibility selectors from `G5.3`.
4. Logs expose `wal_lane_id`, backlog, staged frames, flush trigger, control mode, lane-policy version, shadow verdict, compatibility selector, fallback reason, and elapsed time.
5. The bead demonstrates that ordinary append staging no longer relies on the old centralized append gate while preserving semantics under conservative comparison modes.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-22T23:11:04.785173056Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-11T00:32:30.216892315Z","closed_at":"2026-04-11T00:32:30.216566403Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["contention","implementation","performance","track-d","wal"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3wop3.1.2","depends_on_id":"bd-3wop3.1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-22T23:11:04.785173056Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3wop3.1.2","depends_on_id":"bd-3wop3.1.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-22T23:11:54.741458552Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3wop3.1.2","depends_on_id":"bd-db300.7.5.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-22T23:53:33.934904653Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3wop3.1.2","depends_on_id":"bd-db300.7.5.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-23T00:07:04.267435720Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
-{"id":"bd-3wop3.1.3","title":"D1.c: implement durability combiner, commit certificates, and visibility-publication handoff","description":"## Goal
Implement the minimal ordered durability and publication step that turns locally staged WAL work into a committed, reader-visible result.

## Scope
- assign commit certificates or equivalent deterministic ordering records,
- perform the irreducible ordered durability work,
- publish authoritative visibility metadata that readers and checkpoint logic can bind to directly,
- surface telemetry for batch size, ordered-region time, and fallback entry,
- provide an explicit operator/test control surface for `auto`, conservative/serial publication, and any shadow certificate/publication validation mode.

## Required Verification and Logging
- Unit tests for commit-certificate assignment, ordered-residue sequencing, duplicate or stale publication rejection, reader-visible publication correctness, conservative-mode routing, and shadow validation.
- Integration tests for concurrent lane flushes, checkpoint overlap, visibility binding, control-mode equivalence, and fallback entry.
- Compatibility reruns for the WAL visibility, checkpoint, and read-path surfaces selected by `G5.3`.
- A named e2e script such as `scripts/verify_d1_parallel_wal_publication.sh` that runs canonical append/read/checkpoint scenarios against the new combiner path and emits first-failure bundles.
- Structured logging that includes at minimum `trace_id`, `scenario_id`, `commit_certificate`, `durability_seq`, `publication_generation`, `ordered_region_ns`, `batch_size`, `lookup_mode`, `control_mode`, `shadow_certificate_verdict`, `compatibility_selector`, `fallback_reason`, and first-failure diagnostics.

## Constraints
- This bead must stay tiny in critical-section scope.
- Readers must never need broad reverse-scan-style truth reconstruction after this publication step.
- The design must align with the authoritative WAL visibility work already in the backlog rather than creating a second incompatible truth model.
- Control-mode overrides must preserve user-visible WAL semantics even when they deliberately force the conservative path.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Commit publication produces authoritative visibility metadata with bounded lookup cost.
- The ordered durability region is explicitly measured.
- Matrix artifacts show the combiner is reducing centralized publish cost rather than simply moving it around.
- Conservative/serial publication and shadow validation can be used to compare semantics and diagnostics mechanically.
- Unit, integration, compatibility, and named e2e verification all cover the new publication semantics.","acceptance_criteria":"1. Commit certificates and the ordered durability/publication residue are implemented with deterministic tests.
2. `scripts/verify_d1_parallel_wal_publication.sh` or equivalent named e2e coverage exists, exercises append/read/checkpoint scenarios, and includes the relevant compatibility selectors from `G5.3`.
3. Logs expose commit certificate, durability sequence, publication generation, ordered-region time, batch size, control mode, shadow-certificate verdict, compatibility selector, and fallback reason.
4. Readers can bind to authoritative visibility metadata without broad reverse-scan recovery on the ordinary path, and conservative/shadow modes exist for diagnosis.
5. Before/after artifacts show the combiner is shrinking centralized publication cost rather than relocating it opaquely, while preserving semantics under control-mode comparison.","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-22T23:11:05.416036079Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-23T00:07:07.141724774Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["contention","implementation","performance","publication","track-d","wal"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3wop3.1.3","depends_on_id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.8.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-22T23:11:56.391333673Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3wop3.1.3","depends_on_id":"bd-3wop3.1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-22T23:11:05.416036079Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3wop3.1.3","depends_on_id":"bd-3wop3.1.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-22T23:11:55.296333502Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3wop3.1.3","depends_on_id":"bd-3wop3.1.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-22T23:11:55.840568456Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3wop3.1.3","depends_on_id":"bd-db300.7.5.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-22T23:53:36.353694047Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3wop3.1.3","depends_on_id":"bd-db300.7.5.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-23T00:07:07.140382561Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

4. Readers can bind to authoritative visibility metadata without broad reverse-scan recovery on the ordinary path, and conservative/shadow modes exist for diagnosis.

Before/after artifacts show the combiner is shrinking centralized publication cost rather than relocating it opaquely, while preserving semantics under control-mode comparison.","status":"in_progress","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-22T23:11:05.416036079Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-15T03:57:46.629078371Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["contention","implementation","performance","publication","track-d","wal"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3wop3.1.3","depends_on_id":"bd-1dp9.6.7.8.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-22T23:11:56.391333673Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3wop3.1.3","depends_on_id":"bd-3wop3.1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-22T23:11:05.416036079Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3wop3.1.3","depends_on_id":"bd-3wop3.1.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-22T23:11:55.296333502Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3wop3.1.3","depends_on_id":"bd-3wop3.1.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-22T23:11:55.840568456Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3wop3.1.3","depends_on_id":"bd-db300.7.5.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-22T23:53:36.353694047Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3wop3.1.3","depends_on_id":"bd-db300.7.5.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-23T00:07:07.140382561Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-3wop3.1.4","title":"D1.d: recovery, checkpoint, truncate, and failure-injection proof pack for parallel WAL","description":"## Goal
Prove that the parallel WAL shape is correct under restart and failure, not just fast on a benchmark.

## Scope
Cover:
- crash recovery and replay across multiple lanes or append sources,
- checkpoint merge semantics,
- truncate/reset and generation rollover,
- torn-tail and corruption handling,
- cleanup of stale lane state after abnormal termination,
- conservative-mode replay equivalence and any shadow-validation path used by operators/tests.

## Required Verification and Logging
- Deterministic unit tests for lane replay ordering, certificate continuity, generation rollover, stale-lane cleanup, and control-mode equivalence.
- Integration and fault-injection tests for crash points around append, publication, checkpoint, reset, restart, and conservative-mode fallback.
- Compatibility reruns for the WAL/recovery/checkpoint surfaces selected by `G5.3`.
- A named e2e script such as `scripts/verify_d1_parallel_wal_recovery.sh` that exercises recovery and checkpoint scenarios with fixed seeds and emits first-failure bundles.
- Structured logs and artifact bundles exposing at minimum `trace_id`, `scenario_id`, `wal_lane_id`, `commit_certificate`, `replay_generation`, `checkpoint_phase`, `recovery_mode`, `control_mode`, `shadow_verdict`, `compatibility_selector`, `fallback_reason`, and first-failure diagnostics.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Deterministic unit and integration tests cover the new recovery surface.
- Failure-injection runs exercise crash points around append, publication, checkpoint, and reset.
- Compatibility reruns are green for the affected WAL/recovery surfaces.
- The proof pack names the exact invariants preserved versus the current WAL semantics, including control-mode equivalence.
- No performance claim for the new WAL shape is treated as credible until this bead is green.
- Unit, fault-injection, compatibility, and named e2e recovery verification all exist for the touched path.","acceptance_criteria":"1. The recovery proof pack includes deterministic unit tests, integration/fault-injection tests, compatibility reruns, and `scripts/verify_d1_parallel_wal_recovery.sh` or equivalent named e2e coverage.
2. Artifacts record lane identity, commit certificate continuity, replay generation, checkpoint phase, recovery mode, control mode, shadow verdict, compatibility selector, and fallback context.
3. The proof note states exactly which restart and durability invariants are preserved relative to the current WAL semantics, including conservative-mode equivalence.
4. This bead can veto the parallel WAL design if recovery, compatibility, or diagnosability is not good enough.
5. The bead is self-contained enough that no later agent has to infer the recovery obligations from the implementation alone.","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-22T23:11:06.057388986Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-22T23:53:33.320825658Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["contention","performance","recovery","track-d","verification","wal"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3wop3.1.4","depends_on_id":"bd-3wop3.1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-22T23:11:06.057388986Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3wop3.1.4","depends_on_id":"bd-3wop3.1.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-22T23:29:03.674861187Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3wop3.1.4","depends_on_id":"bd-3wop3.1.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-22T23:11:56.935500871Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3wop3.1.4","depends_on_id":"bd-3wop3.1.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-22T23:11:57.507098161Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3wop3.1.4","depends_on_id":"bd-966ja","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-22T23:17:27.367468700Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3wop3.1.4","depends_on_id":"bd-db300.7.5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-22T23:17:26.830719347Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3wop3.1.4","depends_on_id":"bd-db300.7.5.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-22T23:37:48.752023151Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-3wop3.1.5","title":"D1.e: matrix attribution and regression gate for parallel WAL weak and strong cells","description":"## Goal
Make the parallel WAL work earn its keep across both the weak low-thread cells and the strong many-core cells.

## Scope
Run and archive before/after comparisons for at least:
- `c1`, `c4`, and `c8`,
- single-writer and MVCC modes where relevant,
- the canonical workload families used for the project scorecard,
- failure-free and targeted failure-injection comparison cases where the WAL design changed behavior,
- auto versus conservative/safe-mode control comparisons where needed to expose the real tax or benefit of the new path.

## Required Verification and Reporting
- A named operator entrypoint such as `scripts/verify_d1_parallel_wal_matrix.sh` that runs the agreed benchmark matrix rows, relevant compatibility selectors, and control-mode comparisons, then writes replayable artifact manifests.
- Matrix reports that separate low-thread fixed-cost impact from high-thread scalability impact.
- Regression summaries that include throughput, `p50/p95/p99`, retry or fallback rates, CPU utilization if available, control-mode deltas, and visibility/recovery anomalies if present.
- Structured report logs containing `trace_id`, `scenario_id`, `mode`, `concurrency`, `placement_profile`, `wal_lane_count`, `ordered_region_ns`, `retry_rate`, `fallback_rate`, `control_mode`, `compatibility_selector`, `ops_per_sec`, and first-failure diagnostics.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Artifacts clearly separate low-thread fixed-cost impact from high-thread scalability impact.
- The output names what the new WAL design improved, what it did not, and what regressions remain.
- Auto-versus-conservative comparisons are explicit so a many-core win cannot hide weak-cell or semantics tax.
- Compatibility status for the affected WAL surfaces is recorded alongside the performance report.
- This bead is sufficient to block hand-wavy claims that a many-core WAL win justifies worse weak-cell behavior.
- Results are wired into the existing contention regression gate instead of living in isolation.
- The named matrix script, report bundle, and structured logs are good enough for another agent to reproduce the attribution pack mechanically.","acceptance_criteria":"1. `scripts/verify_d1_parallel_wal_matrix.sh` or equivalent named matrix entrypoint exists and emits replayable artifacts for weak and strong cells, plus relevant compatibility selectors and control-mode comparisons.
2. Reports include throughput, latency, retry/fallback behavior, placement context, ordered-region cost, control-mode deltas, and compatibility status.
3. Structured logs expose mode, concurrency, placement profile, lane count, ordered-region time, retry/fallback rates, control mode, compatibility selector, and first-failure diagnostics.
4. The bead explicitly states whether the new WAL design improved weak cells, strong cells, both, or neither, and what semantic or operational tax remains.
5. The output is wired into `bd-3wop3.7` and the broader scorecard so the result can gate future claims.","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-22T23:11:06.718556722Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-23T00:16:34.901277359Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["benchmark","contention","performance","track-d","verification","wal"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3wop3.1.5","depends_on_id":"bd-3wop3.1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-22T23:11:06.718556722Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3wop3.1.5","depends_on_id":"bd-3wop3.1.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-22T23:30:00.975777604Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3wop3.1.5","depends_on_id":"bd-3wop3.1.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-22T23:11:58.091457750Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3wop3.1.5","depends_on_id":"bd-3wop3.1.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-22T23:11:58.688149641Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3wop3.1.5","depends_on_id":"bd-db300.7.5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-22T23:17:28.027260370Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3wop3.1.5","depends_on_id":"bd-db300.7.5.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-22T23:37:50.482351830Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3wop3.1.5","depends_on_id":"bd-db300.7.5.5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-23T00:16:31.207045204Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3wop3.1.5","depends_on_id":"bd-db300.7.5.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-23T00:16:34.899961502Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3wop3.1.5","depends_on_id":"bd-db300.7.8","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-22T23:11:59.451443825Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-3wop3.2","title":"D2: Shard PageCache into N partitions (PostgreSQL BufMappingPartitionLock pattern)","description":"## The #2 Bottleneck

PageCache is behind a single Mutex<PageCache> (pager.rs:1008). With 8+ threads doing thousands of page reads/writes, all 8000+ operations serialize through one lock.

Current code:
  cache: Arc<Mutex<PageCache>>,
  // Acquired ~35 times across pager.rs for get_page, put_page, evict, dirty, etc.

## Solution: Sharded PageCache (PostgreSQL Pattern)

PostgreSQL solved this decades ago with BufMappingPartitionLock: partition the buffer pool hash table into N shards, each with its own lock.

### Architecture

1. Replace Mutex<PageCache> with ShardedPageCache { shards: [CachePadded<Mutex<PageCacheShard>>; 128] }
2. PageNumber hashing: shard_index = (pgno.get() as usize) % 128
3. Each shard owns its own HashMap<PageNumber, PageEntry> + LRU/S3-FIFO list
4. Operations that need cross-shard coordination (global eviction pressure) use a separate AtomicU64 counter

### Key Design Decisions

- 128 shards: 16x the thread count, so even at 16 threads, contention per shard is ~0.125 threads
- CachePadded: prevents false sharing between adjacent shard locks (64-byte alignment)
- Eviction: local per-shard eviction first; global eviction only under extreme memory pressure
- Pin counting: per-shard pin counter (AtomicU32 per shard, not global)

### Why This Works

- Converts one hot lock into 128 cold locks
- At 8 threads with 128 shards, probability of two threads hitting same shard = 8/128 = 6.25%
- PostgreSQL has shipped this pattern since 2005; proven at 100+ cores

### Files to Modify
- crates/fsqlite-pager/src/pager.rs — ShardedPageCache replacing single Mutex<PageCache>
- crates/fsqlite-pager/src/s3_fifo.rs — per-shard eviction policy

### Testing
1. test_sharded_cache_8t_no_deadlock — 8 threads, random page access, no deadlock
2. test_sharded_cache_16t_throughput — 16 threads, measure ops/sec, assert > 2x single-lock
3. test_shard_distribution_uniform — 10K random pages, verify even shard distribution
4. test_cross_shard_eviction — global pressure triggers eviction across shards
5. test_cache_line_padding — static_assert ShardedPageCache shard alignment = 64 bytes
6. test_pin_count_per_shard — concurrent pin/unpin on different shards don't interfere

### Baseline Comparator
Current: Single Mutex<PageCache> (pager.rs:1008)
Target: 128-shard CachePadded<Mutex<PageCacheShard>>","status":"in_progress","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"ubuntu","estimated_minutes":720,"created_at":"2026-03-20T18:31:57.480996015Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-23T08:22:03.259122594Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["contention","pager","phase1","track-d"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3wop3.2","depends_on_id":"bd-3wop3","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-20T18:31:57.480996015Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":2495,"issue_id":"bd-3wop3.2","author":"ubuntu","text":"D2 COMPLETED: ShardedPageCache fully integrated into SimplePager/SimpleTransaction/SimplePagerCheckpointWriter. All 13 unit tests pass including 8/16-thread deadlock and throughput tests. Workspace compiles, clippy clean. Key changes: (1) Replaced Arc<Mutex<PageCache>> with Arc<ShardedPageCache>, (2) Removed all .lock() calls since ShardedPageCache uses per-shard internal locks, (3) Fixed patch_page1_header to take &ShardedPageCache.","created_at":"2026-03-20T18:59:44Z"}]}
